sentences.text: Yeah.
I remember my grandfather.
There was a shed in the back of the house that we lived in.
And at night, my father and my grandfather used to dig in the shed.
And I was very curious about what they were doing, particularly at night.
And I found out that they were putting a lot of valuables underground, because they knew that the good times-- if you want to call them-- that weren't going to last.

So they were-- I can just see my father and grandfather digging a ditch about six feet deep and putting all the valuable silver and all kinds of things, candelabras, putting it in there, and then covering it up, wrapping everything in cloth.
They hoped to return someday?
Yes.
We were-- we were directed.
We were made to go to an army-- an air force, a German air force base.
It was called Wsola, W-S-O-L-A.
From the ghetto.
I'm not that good with dates.
I would say it was in the neighborhood of 1940 or so.
Yeah.
As a matter of fact, the whole family.
My father was a skilled mechanic.
And the Germans planned to make good use of him.
And he worked as a mechanic at the air force base, at the army base.
And I worked as an electrician's helper.
I don't remember.
But I assume it was by horse cart.
It's-- it was only several miles, something like 10 miles or so from Jedlisnk.
And we stayed there.
It-- the life wasn't bad.
It-- there was a communal kitchen.
My mother worked in the kitchen.
I mean, comparatively speaking, it was not a bad deal.
I was carrying-- I was carrying the things for the electricians.
He was teaching me the negative from the positive wires, so I wouldn't electrocute myself.
And I still know how to wire a house, if necessary.
It was communal.
It wasn't bad.
But there were cots.
And the women lived in one part of the building, and the men lived in another part of the building.
But we saw each other at meal times, like at dinner time.
And conditions were not bad.
But at the same time, another piece of our freedom was abrogated.
Another piece of the salami was cut.
He-- they still had talks with my mother and with other people who were there.
They were trying not to sound revolutionary, because the Germans wouldn't allow that.
We were not abused badly.
Occasionally, somebody would get beaten up.
But in general, they left us more or less alone.
It was just forced work.
And then one day, they transported us.
They put us on trains, on cattle cars, and transported us to a place called Pionki.
P-I-O-N-K-I.
It was some place in Poland.
And it was a huge factory.
By that, I mean it covered miles and miles.
It was a munitions factory.
I don't know.
Probably in the very early '40s, like '41 or so.
The trip was extremely unpleasant.
But I was there with my father and mother, so it wasn't as bad as it could have been.
It was-- it was very exciting for me, mostly unpleasant.
But it wasn't anything that I couldn't live with.
There were about 100 or so, possibly more.
It was pretty crowded.
But we were slowly getting used to abuse.
Several.
It took several days to get to Pionki, as far as I can remember.
Now, coming to Pionki was a real revelation.
I started-- they assigned me to work in a place called [NON-ENGLISH], which means the packing and shipping.
And it was-- I was still, at the time, a little, fat, spoiled kid, about nine years old, 10 years old.
And they started working me extremely hard over there.
I would say that within six weeks, I probably lost a third of my weight.
And I became lean and not so mean.
I was working very hard.
Like we were made to carry-- let's see, like-- like 40 kilos, which is about 100-pound sacks of gunpowder on our backs.
And I became very adept at those things.
Either you do it or you die.
It was-- there was no-- there was no question about it.
Like for instance, I also had an aunt who worked there, and both my mother and father.
My father worked as a mechanic.
He had the best work.
My mother worked in another part of a gunpowder factory.
And they had-- the buildings were-- the factory buildings were separated by quite a lot of space.
I mean, it seems like hundreds of yards of space.
Because there were explosions fairly close by.
And one day, I was told that my mother had died.
She was burned alive in one of the explosions.
And I worked in shipping, for instance.
And my-- my father was always telling me, stay out of trouble.
Stay out of trouble.
And in order to make gunpowder, they used alcohol.
They used, as a matter of fact, a very, very strong alcohol, something like 196 proof, 98% alcohol.
And we learned to drink that stuff.
Occasionally, it was laced with ether.
And if you drank it straight without diluting it with water, you could either go blind or die.
Also, it was against the rules to drink any of that stuff.
But most of us were walking around drunk most of the time.
About 10.
I was one of the youngest alcoholics around.
And if they caught you drunk or drinking that stuff-- as a matter of fact, we had to siphon it out of the-- they had railroad cars full of that stuff.
And we learned how to bleed some of it off.

If they caught you with any of this alcohol, or they caught you drunk, on Sundays-- which was our day off-- they had a ceremony where they hanged everybody who was caught [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] drinking.
No.
I mean, just as it didn't deter me from getting food any place I could, it didn't deter me from drinking much.
The main thing was don't get caught.
As a matter of fact, one of the incidents that happened over there, another guy and I, it-- they had a narrow gauge railroad in Pionki at that factory.
And another guy and I were transporting, pushing, a cart with a bunch of cart-- with a bunch of boxes of gunpowder.
And it had a brake on it that-- but the brake-- the brakes failed.
And I knew that if this thing crashed into-- if this wagon crashed into anything, and it exploded, we'd both be executed.
So I tried to stop it from the front.
He ran.
I tried to stop it.
Finally, it ran over me and it-- my leg, my left, upper thigh, stopped it.
And I was praised for it, and I was given the rest of the day off.
I remember I got a hold of two potatoes.
And since I had the day off, I cooked them in one of those dilapidated jars.
And I had me a feast.
And when I came back-- when I came back to work the following day-- I was at the dispensary.
They put a bunch of some junk on my leg.
It was hurting like hell.
But in that place, we were all-- they also had Polish Gentiles working, who came, who were day workers.
They came to work, and then they went home in the evening.
From some of them, we used to buy food.
They would bring us food.
But when I came back the next day, everybody wanted to look at my leg.
And they started-- they started making fun of me, like [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which means in-- which in Polish means the railroad car chopped off your testicles.
So that was-- that was a lot of fun.
Not very badly.
I still have a scar on my left thigh.
The following day.
But it wasn't-- it wasn't a matter of wanting to do anything.
It was either you do it or you don't-- or you die.
The food-- the food was minimal.
But at the time that we were working with the-- with the Gentiles, we used to buy food from them.
Like let's say once a week they would bring a loaf of bread, and I would pay them.
Sometimes they'd bring a piece of-- a slab of bacon, which wasn't exactly kosher.
But I really didn't care about that.
It tasted great.
Yes, at this point, my family still had money.
And I remember we had-- when they moved us from Pionki, they made us empty-- they had some kind of cellars over there.
I don't know for what reason.
But they had cheeses, sort of round cheeses in this cellar.
And while we were loading those cheeses into trucks, we would break some of them and eat them.
I hadn't been able to eat or smell cheese for years afterwards.
It smelled very ripe.
Well, they are, aside from our ration, whatever we could get, we could buy from the other workers.
And then from there, from there, it all went downhill.
Because the next stop was Auschwitz.
No.
They were not prisoners.
They got paid for their work.
And they-- they also abused us, but not in any way that we couldn't live with.
I mean, there were Germans who were in charge of the camp.
But a lot of the administration and a lot of the supervisory work was done by-- by the Polish people.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I can remember where, if one of the Pollocks told you to do something, you better do it, or they reported you to a higher up.
And you would get punished, and the punishment was very-- it-- it-- it was-- it was strict.
Usually the Germans.
Sometimes the Poles, and sometimes the Jews.
Yeah.
Well, they had-- they had a sort of a-- they had trusties, sort of like kapos, except we didn't call them kapos over there.
There were-- Jewish people were put in charge.
You are going to be a policeman.
You're going to have to watch out for these people.
If they don't do what they're told to do, you will punish them.
So you whack somebody over the back or over the head with a stick.
Or-- and power corrupts.
There were Jewish people in charge of much of that power.
I don't know.
Most of them-- most of them seemed to go about it as if it was their life's work.
By this time, it started getting to be every man for himself.
And there's another thing I think I should mention.
Like, for instance, by that time, we knew that our grandparents, that the older people, the people who didn't-- not wind up in camps, wound up in places like Treblinka, which were death camps.
And there were-- they had all been gassed and killed.
The rumor mill.
Nobody-- nobody gave us videos or pictures.
We knew some of them, like Majdanek, Treblinka.
There were-- there were rumors going around.
Of course, a lot of people didn't want to believe it.
I mean, oh, how-- how could you believe in atrocities like this and senseless barbarism or senseless killing of older people and children and just killing of everybody?
We were-- we were civilized people, and we assumed that most other people were civilized also.
And in spite of the fact that the Polish people didn't treat us like friends-- occasionally, they had a pogrom and beat up and possibly killed some people.
But we're talking about wholesale slaughter, where my grandparents were sent.
My father and I both sat there and cried through the night.
As a matter of fact, I remember one of the-- one of the-- one of the-- my Polish co-workers said, hey, Henjek, I hear your mother was barbecued, things like that.
They told us we are leaving.
As a matter of fact, they told us we're leaving because they were going to take us to Germany to another work camp.
The rumor mills were always strong, and they were telling us-- the rumor mills were telling us, nothing was officially announced-- we're going to Germany.
They're going to put us to-- in a good work camp where they're giving you decent food and all that.
And most people actually believed that.
I said-- I believed it anyway.
But then I was a child of 10.
And they put us in-- in real wagons, in railroad cars that were closed up, about 200, 300 people to the car.
They sealed them, and it took us about a week or more to get to Auschwitz.
It was pretty miserable.
Some people--
Oh yes, definitely.
I mean, things started getting-- they-- they never went uphill.
They always went downhill.
That was the law of concentration camp.
Things will never get better.
They'll always get worse, and you can expect that.
They-- they-- they-- they behaved like animals.
They had some kind of buckets, and they threw them out through the window.
Every couple of days, they stopped and let-- and to take out some of the dead people who had died.
Yeah.
They gave-- they gave us some water and some bread.
But-- and also, I think I-- that's the last time I got sick in concentration camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I think I was sick for several days during the trip.
If it hadn't been for my father, I wouldn't have survived it.
Yes, my father and I.
And my father was despondent, and he tried to keep my spirits up.
He says, we'll get over this.
We'll get over this.
Yeah.
I usually believed my father.
He was-- he was believable.
And he was a strong man, and he was a good mechanic.
And when he told me something, I wanted to believe him.
When we arrived in Auschwitz, it was one of those nightmarish things where there was a band-- there was an orchestra playing.
And every-- they started beating up on us.
The guards started beating up on us to get out of the cars.
And the band playing Strauss music and waltzes and all kinds of other things.
And there was a selection.
That's the first time I had experienced a selection, where several Germans were sitting at the table.
And they were pointing to either to the left or to the right.
At the time, we were still wearing our own clothes.
And there were older people, and there were some people younger than I.
And at the time, I made-- my father told me to make myself as tall as I possibly could.
And I was fairly robust and tall for my age.
And the people, let's say, who went to the left went to the crematorium, to the gas chambers.
And the ones who went to the right went to-- to the wonderful camp of Auschwitz-Birkenau.
Yes.
Yes.
As a matter of fact, that was probably the first time when-- that I was completely terrified.
And the terror didn't stop for several years.
I was-- I mean, if I could have, I would have shaken with fright.
Yes, I walked with my father.
And every-- they were always watching us to see if they had missed any infirmity or anything.
And then they looked.
They checked my father's papers, which we never kept.
But they kept track of us.
And they found out he was a mechanic and I was a skilled mechanics helper.
And then they took us to-- out to the Auschwitz camp.
And before they took us to camp, we had to-- they stripped our clothes and all our possessions.
And that's the last time I ever saw any of my clothes or possessions.
Some striped things.
Except I got to keep my shoes.
And that's why I'd-- I-- none of our possessions or even pictures survived the camps.
Yeah.
And then as we were processed, before they gave us the uniforms, they put us through a, what they called, the delousing operation.
They put us in a shower, several hundred people of us.
And while we were waiting for the water-- and there were about a dozen shower heads.
And while we were waiting for the shower to come on, the rumor started.
This is not really a shower.
It's a gas chamber.
It's just a sort of a dummy shower.
And they threw in one piece of soap-- or two pieces of soap.
And some of our great jokers-- there was-- there were a lot of jokers that would say, be careful with that soap.
I hear the Germans are rendering-- rendering people, and that this soap may contain parts of your grandmother.
I-- I wasn't any more terrified than I was outside.
I mean, terror can only go so far.
After that, after we showered and washed, there were a bunch of prisoners sitting there and with tattoo equipment and sort of needles.
And they started tattooing.
And my number was B21.
Yes.
Can you see it?
We were assigned to barracks.
I don't know.
It must have been in the early '40s.
I was in Auschwitz only a fairly short time, only several months, which seemed actually like years.
And in Auschwitz, they had a-- let's say we were in a-- in one block, which is sort of like like an army barracks.
And it had cots, some three high, some four high.
And those things were not too clean.
Some of them had straw, and some of them didn't.
Some of them just had a blanket.
And during the-- during the day, this was not a work camp.
We only had work gangs.
Like they grabbed you in the morning, throw you on a truck, you're going to work.
And during the day, they had also trucks patrolling the camp.
It was like a regular street with houses, which were the barracks on both sides.
And they would-- they would ride around in a truck and grab people, threw them on the truck, beat up on them, and took them to the gas chambers.
I had-- I had a little-- a friend, who was, I think, a year or so-- at least he looked about a year or two younger than I.
And I saw him thrown on the truck.
And he jumped off the truck and started running.
And they grabbed them again.
They beat up on him, threw him on the truck.
He jumped off the truck again.
He had more spirit than anybody I ever saw.
He was gassed.
Yes.
Occasionally they took us on work gangs.
For instance, one time, I-- the rumor had it that some of my father's two sisters were in the women's camp.
Now, the Germans separated the sexes very strictly.
And I-- I found-- I found a way to go on a work detail to the women's camp.
I think we were doing some kind of plumbing or something.
By the way, they-- they didn't have any real plumbing.
They had outhouses that really stunk to high heaven.
And I got-- I actually got to see my father's two sisters.
One-- one looked very much abused.
And I-- I was a cocky kid.
I asked her who was abusing her, and she told me that her blockalteste, who was also a kapoo, a trusty prisoner.
And I asked her to show me which one it was, and she showed me.
And I went over to her, and in my best German, arrogant language, I told her that-- I told her if she abused my aunt, I'll find a way of getting her killed.
